courses.title: Microeconomics
courses.blurb: An introduction to microeconomic principles — consumer choice and demand, firm behaviour and costs, and competitive markets — with lecture slides and weekly problem sets.
courses.intro: An introduction to microeconomic principles — consumer choice and demand, firm behaviour and costs, and competitive markets. Each session pairs lecture slides with a problem set for practice. Materials are added as the course runs.

sections.title: Week 1
sections.note: Foundations of microeconomics: consumer choice, demand, firm behaviour, and competitive markets.

items.label: Day 1
items.title: Ten Principles of Economics & Thinking Like an Economist
items.summary: What economics is: the ten principles, opportunity cost and marginal thinking, the production possibilities frontier, and the gains from trade through comparative advantage.

items.label: Day 2
items.title: The Market Forces of Supply and Demand
items.summary: How markets work: demand and supply, market equilibrium, and the comparative statics of price and quantity.

items.label: Day 3
items.title: Elasticity and Its Application
items.summary: Price elasticity of demand, its link to total revenue, other elasticities (income and cross-price), and common pitfalls.

items.label: Day 4
items.title: Supply, Demand, Government Policies, and the Efficiency of Markets
items.summary: Price controls, consumer and producer surplus, market efficiency, and the deadweight loss of taxes.

sections.title: Week 2
sections.note: From the cost curves to market structure: costs of production, perfect competition, monopoly, and duopoly.

items.label: Day 1
items.title: Costs of Production
items.summary: Fixed, variable and total cost; the three per-unit curves (average fixed, average variable and average total cost) and marginal cost; the averaging rule that links marginal to average cost; and the distinction between short-run costs and the long-run average cost curve.

items.label: Day 2
items.title: Perfect Competition
items.summary: The four features of a competitive market and why marginal revenue equals average revenue equals price for a price-taker; the profit-maximising rule MR = MC; the short-run shut-down rule when price fails to cover average variable cost; and long-run entry and exit that drive profit to zero.

items.label: Day 3
items.title: Monopoly
items.summary: Sources of market power and why marginal revenue lies below price (the MR line has twice the slope of demand); the monopolist's MR = MC output, with lower quantity and higher price than under competition; the resulting deadweight loss; and price discrimination, first-degree and third-degree across separate markets.

items.label: Day 4
items.title: Duopoly: Two Firms
items.summary: Bertrand price competition and the pressure to undercut; Cournot quantity competition solved through reaction functions to a Nash equilibrium; the Stackelberg first-mover advantage; and a comparison of the four market structures on a common example.

sections.title: Week 3
sections.note: Strategic behaviour and market failure: choosing when the best move depends on what others do, cooperation sustained by repetition, and the costs and benefits that spill onto third parties.

items.label: Day 1
items.title: Introduction to Game Theory
items.summary: How people and firms make choices when the best move depends on what others do: normal-form games, dominant strategies, and Nash equilibrium.

items.label: Day 2
items.title: Repeated Games
items.summary: Cooperation collapsed in one shot. When rivals meet again and again, the future can rescue it.

items.label: Day 3
items.title: Externalities, Public Goods, and the Commons
items.summary: When a market gets the quantity wrong because costs or benefits spill onto others: marginal private versus marginal social cost, the efficient quantity, and corrective taxes.

courses.title: Intermediate Microeconomics
courses.blurb: Intermediate microeconomics covering consumer and producer theory, market structure, and welfare, with lecture slides and weekly problem sets.
courses.intro: Consumer and producer theory, market structure, and welfare. Each session pairs lecture slides with a problem set for practice. Materials are added as the course runs.

sections.title: Week 1
sections.note: Building the demand and supply curves from primitives across a five-day arc: preferences, optimisation, demand, production, and cost & supply.

items.label: Day 1
items.title: Preferences and Utility Functions
items.summary: What the consumer wants: indifference curves, the marginal rate of substitution, and utility as an ordinal representation of preferences. (Perloff Ch. 3)

items.label: Day 2
items.title: The Constrained Optimisation Problem
items.summary: What the consumer can afford: the budget line and solving max u(x, y) subject to the budget to pin down the chosen bundle. (Perloff Ch. 4)

items.label: Day 3
items.title: Demand: Hicks, Slutsky, and Elasticity
items.summary: Marshallian vs Hicksian (compensated) demand and the expenditure function, the Slutsky substitution/income decomposition, and elasticities. (Perloff Ch. 5, §2.3)

items.label: Day 4
items.title: Firms, Production, and Production Functions
items.summary: Crossing to the supply side: the firm's technology — production functions, isoquants, MRTS — and its structural parallel with consumer theory. (Perloff Ch. 6)

items.label: Day 5
items.title: Costs and the Firm's Supply Curve
items.summary: Input prices and cost minimisation, the short-run cost curves, and profit maximisation to derive the supply curve — completing the demand–supply diagram from first principles. (Perloff Ch. 7, §8.1–8.4)

sections.title: Week 2
sections.note: Putting the Week 1 tools to work on a single market: supply and demand and equilibrium, welfare and taxation, the competitive firm, and monopoly.

items.label: Day 1
items.title: Supply, Demand, and Market Equilibrium
items.summary: Movements along a curve versus shifts of the curve and their ceteris-paribus content, solving for equilibrium algebraically and on a diagram, the comparative statics of demand and supply shocks, and point elasticities along a linear demand curve. (Perloff Ch. 2)

items.label: Day 2
items.title: Welfare and Tax Incidence
items.summary: Consumer and producer surplus as a measure of the gains from trade and why the competitive equilibrium is efficient, binding price ceilings and floors and their deadweight loss, and the incidence of a sales tax — pass-through, revenue, and who bears the burden. (Perloff Chs. 8–9)

items.label: Day 3
items.title: Competitive Firms and Markets
items.summary: The price-taking firm's profit problem (P = MC), building the MC, AVC and AC curves and locating the shutdown and break-even prices, deriving the firm's supply curve and aggregating it to the market, and long-run entry and exit to the zero-profit equilibrium. (Perloff Ch. 8)

items.label: Day 4
items.title: Monopoly and Market Power
items.summary: A single firm facing market demand: why marginal revenue is below price, the MR = MC output and its comparison with the competitive benchmark, the surplus transfer and deadweight loss via the Lerner index, and first- and third-degree price discrimination. (Perloff Chs. 11–12)

sections.title: Week 3
sections.note: Strategic interaction: from the three duopoly models to the general language of game theory, repetition and cooperation, and the market failures that survive competition.

items.label: Day 1
items.title: Duopoly: Bertrand, Cournot, and Stackelberg
items.summary: One market, three ways two firms can compete — and where each lands between monopoly and perfect competition.

items.label: Day 2
items.title: Introduction to Game Theory
items.summary: From "mutual best replies" in duopoly to a general language for strategic choice: normal-form games, dominance, and Nash equilibrium.

items.label: Day 3
items.title: Repeated Games
items.summary: The Prisoner's Dilemma said cooperation is doomed. Repetition — and the shadow of the future — can rescue it.

items.label: Day 4
items.title: Externalities, Public Goods, and the Commons
items.summary: When even a competitive market gets it wrong: costs and benefits that spill onto others, and the goods the market under-provides.

courses.title: Advanced Financial Econometrics
courses.blurb: An introduction to the econometrics of financial markets: asset returns, linear time-series and volatility models, derivative pricing, and tail-risk measures, with applied R supplements on real market data.
courses.intro: An introduction to the econometrics of financial markets: asset returns and their distributional properties, linear time-series models (ARMA), volatility modelling (GARCH), continuous-time processes for derivative pricing, and tail-risk measures (EVT and Value-at-Risk). Each chapter pairs lecture slides with a hands-on R supplement on real data.

links.label: Read the course book
links.note: Time Series Analysis in Financial Econometrics — a hands-on guide with R and Python, following Tsay. Fifteen chapters covering returns, ARMA, unit roots, ARCH/GARCH, nonlinear models, continuous-time processes, options, and extreme values.

items.label: Week 1 · Chapter 1
items.title: Introduction to Financial Econometrics
items.summary: Asset returns and the distributional properties of returns: simple vs log returns, fat tails, skewness, and non-normality tested on real market data.

items.label: Week 1 · Chapter 2
items.title: Linear Time Series Analysis and Its Applications
items.summary: Stationarity, serial correlation, ARMA modelling, model diagnostics, and forecasting with real data.

items.label: Week 2 · Chapter 3
items.title: Conditional Heteroskedasticity: ARCH & GARCH
items.summary: Volatility clustering and time-varying variance: the ARCH and GARCH families, estimation, diagnostics, and volatility forecasting on real return series.

items.label: Week 2 · Chapter 3
items.title: GARCH Extensions and Model Implementation
items.summary: Practical GARCH modelling workflow: specification choices, estimation, interpretation of volatility persistence, and implementation notes for empirical financial data.

items.label: Week 2 · Chapter 3
items.title: GARCH Variants: IGARCH, GARCH-M, EGARCH
items.summary: Variant models and regime-switching preview, with empirical implementation files for reproducible S&P 500 results.

items.label: Week 2 · Chapter 4
items.title: Nonlinear Time Series Models
items.summary: Threshold and regime-switching frameworks for financial time series, including motivation, model structure, and interpretation.

items.label: Week 3 · Chapter 5
items.title: Extreme Values, Quantiles, and Value at Risk
items.summary: Measuring the risk in the tail of financial returns: turning the course's recurring finding — that returns are fat-tailed — into a risk number, through Value at Risk and Expected Shortfall.

items.label: Week 3 · Chapter 6
items.title: Options: Contracts, Payoffs, and the Greeks
items.summary: From the right to trade, to the risk you hedge. An option is a contract whose value depends above all on volatility — the quantity the rest of the course has spent its time modelling. What an option is and the four building-block positions, what it is worth at expiry and before, and the Greeks.

items.label: Week 3
items.title: Mock Final Examination
items.summary: Full mock final paper — 2 hours, 100 marks — covering the whole module.

items.label: Week 3
items.title: Problem Set 5 — Extreme Values, Quantiles, and Value at Risk
items.summary: Three exercises covering the extreme-value lectures: RiskMetrics and the risk measures, the block-maxima (GEV) approach and return levels, and clustered extremes. Full worked solutions are included at the end.

sections.title: Data
sections.note: Datasets used in the R supplements.
